glossary.def: The relationship between the yield on default-free debt and its maturity — equivalently the discount curve of zero-coupon bond prices P(t,T). It is usually upward-sloping, occasionally flat, and inverts (short rates above long rates) ahead of recessions; reproducing all of these shapes from a small parameter set is the goal of every model in this chapter.

The instantaneous risk-free interest rate r_t for borrowing over the next infinitesimal moment — the front end of the yield curve and the single quantity a short-rate model evolves stochastically. From its dynamics the whole curve follows as a risk-neutral expectation of the discount factor.

The tendency of a process to be pulled back toward a long-run level whenever it strays, via a drift proportional to the gap (level minus current value). In short-rate models the reversion speed a sets the pull strength and 1/a its time constant, so a shock decays with half-life ln2/a.

A 1977 short-rate model in which r_t is an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process: mean-reverting drift a(b−r) plus constant Gaussian noise σ dW. It is affine, giving closed-form bond prices, but its Gaussian terminal law assigns positive probability to negative rates.

A 1985 short-rate model that multiplies Vasicek's noise by √r_t, so volatility vanishes as the rate approaches zero and the rate can never go negative. Its terminal distribution is non-central chi-squared, and it captures the empirical link between the level of rates and their volatility.

The CIR inequality 2ab ≥ σ², under which the short rate stays strictly positive and never even touches zero. If it is violated the rate can reach zero and reflect, but the square-root diffusion still keeps it non-negative.

The extended-Vasicek model dr = (θ(t) − a r)dt + σ dW, in which the drift target θ(t) is a time-dependent function chosen to fit today's observed yield curve exactly. This no-arbitrage construction retains Gaussian tractability — closed-form bonds, bond options, and swaptions — and is the production workhorse of interest-rate desks.

A model whose zero-coupon bond price has the form P(t,T) = A(t,T) e^{−B(t,T) r_t}, making the yield an affine (linear-plus-constant) function of the short rate. Vasicek, CIR and Hull–White are all affine, which is precisely why each admits a closed-form bond price obtained from Riccati ODEs.

refs.cite: Vasicek, O. (1977). An Equilibrium Characterization of the Term Structure. Journal of Financial Economics 5(2).
Cox, J. C., Ingersoll, J. E. & Ross, S. A. (1985). A Theory of the Term Structure of Interest Rates. Econometrica 53(2).
Hull, J. & White, A. (1990). Pricing Interest-Rate-Derivative Securities. Review of Financial Studies 3(4).
Jamshidian, F. (1989). An Exact Bond Option Formula. Journal of Finance 44(1).
Heath, D., Jarrow, R. & Morton, A. (1992). Bond Pricing and the Term Structure of Interest Rates: A New Methodology. Econometrica 60(1).
Brigo, D. & Mercurio, F. (2006). Interest Rate Models — Theory and Practice (2nd ed.). Springer Finance.
